title: Future Logging Careers Act
plain_summary: This bill lets 16 and 17 year olds work dangerous logging jobs if their parents or guardians own the logging business. Right now, these teens cannot do this type of work because it is too dangerous. The bill explains what kinds of logging work this includes, like cutting down trees and using big machines. It only applies when the teen is working for their family's logging company, not for other businesses.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Future Logging Careers Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Child Labor Law Exemptions for Logging Operations
sections.text: This section changes the Fair Labor Standards Act of nineteen thirty-eight. It adds new definitions and rules about young workers in logging jobs. First, it defines what a logging operation means. A logging operation includes using machines to cut down trees and turn timber into logs, poles, fence posts, and similar wood products. It also includes collecting, moving, loading, and transporting these wood products. Building and fixing roads, camps, and equipment used for logging work also counts as part of a logging operation. However, using chainsaws by hand to cut trees or using cable skidders to move timber does not count as a logging operation under this law. The section also defines what a mechanized operation means. This is when machines other than hand-operated chainsaws or cable skidders are used to cut, move, or process timber. This includes using special machines like tree processors, delimbers, feller-bunchers, forwarders, chippers, grinders, debarkers, skidders, yarders, bulldozers, excavators, and log loaders. Finally, the section changes the child labor rules. It says that sixteen and seventeen year old workers can be employed in logging operations, even in jobs that the Secretary of Labor says are particularly dangerous for children their age. However, this only applies when the young person works for their parent or someone acting as their parent in a logging business that the parent owns or runs.
